Machine Learning · Text Analysis · Regression

Predicting Virality of
Urban Dictionary Terms

Sarah Feuer

Introduction

My family and friends will be the first to tell you that I shorten every word I possibly can. Syllables quickly get sliced off my sentences and the people close to me quickly get dubbed with a spunky nickname. Why not? it saves time and adds spice to any mundane conversation. The avid authors of Urban Dictionary are cut from the same cloth, and I admire their witty word play.

Urban Dictionary is an online crowdsourced dictionary that catalogs slang terms and cultural references, providing user-generated definitions that often capture informal, humorous, and niche phrases used mainly in online subcultures. Not to mention, Urban Dictionary words can – and have – broken into the mainstream, changing a whole generation's cultural conversation. What could be seen as silly Urban Dictionary terms, can transform into powerful words used to talk about political campaigns, social movements, and funny memes.

The central question: What makes a "successful" Urban Dictionary word that appeals to the masses? That's the journey we'll embark on together.

Project Outline

The aim of this exploration is to predict our response variable – the number of upvotes an Urban Dictionary word would receive – based on categories outlined below. To build our final regression model, we will:

1. Tidy data & perform EDA  →  2. Train/test split & recipe  →  3. 10-fold cross validation  →  4. Fit 5 models  →  5. Evaluate best model on test set

Exploring The Data (EDA)

Loading and Exploring the Data

The data set that will serve as the building block for this exploration is The Kaggle dataset, "Urban Dictionary Terms" by author ATHONTZ which can be found here. Here's one entry for reference:

worddefinitionauthortagsupdowndate
2trump basketA toilet bowl.jknightx[]729634March 23, 2017
 
dim(urban_dictionary_data)
## [1] 4272    7

Codebook

The data set consists of 4272 observations and 7 variables: word, definition, author, tags, up, down, and date.

4,272Total Entries
7Variables
↑ upResponse Variable

Looking ahead towards model construction: (1) number of tags, (2) whether the word/definition/tags include political terms, and (3) whether the word/definition/tags include explicit terms will serve as insightful predictors. First, we drop author, down, and date.

drop <- c("author", "down", "date")
urban_dictionary_data = urban_dictionary_data[,!(names(urban_dictionary_data) %in% drop)]

Describing the Predictors

I sourced lists of common words and phrases from Glossary of Political Terms and Ultimate Curse Word Bracket. The political list has 180 words and the explicit list has 65.

# add a column if the term is political or not
urban_dictionary_data <- urban_dictionary_data %>%
  mutate(
    word = tolower(word),
    definition = tolower(definition),
    political = ifelse(grepl(paste(politics_list, collapse = "|"), word) |
                       grepl(paste(politics_list, collapse = "|"), definition), TRUE, FALSE)
  )

# add a column if the term is explicit or not
urban_dictionary_data <- urban_dictionary_data %>%
  mutate(
    explicit = ifelse(grepl(paste(explicit_list, collapse = "|"), word) |
                      grepl(paste(explicit_list, collapse = "|"), definition), TRUE, FALSE)
  )
475Political Terms
1,178Explicit Terms
~75%Entries With Tags
# add a column if the term has hashtags or not
urban_dictionary_data <- urban_dictionary_data %>%
  mutate(has_tags = ifelse(tags == "[]", FALSE, TRUE))

A little over 3000 – nearly 75% of our data – has tags!

worddefinitiontagsuppoliticalexplicithas_tags
2trump basketa toilet bowl.[]729TRUEFALSEFALSE
3wealth careexcellent health care reserved exclusively for the wealthy.[u'#insurance', u'#rich', u'#america', ...]1072TRUEFALSETRUE

Visual EDA

Missing Data Plot

Even though it's unlikely there are missing values (data is scraped from Urban Dictionary), it's important to check for missingness.

No missing values across all variables. 🎉

Distribution of Upvotes

ggplot(urban_dictionary_data, aes(x = up)) +
  geom_histogram(fill = "skyblue", color = "black") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Upvotes", x = "Upvotes", y = "Frequency")

Upvotes Summary Statistics

##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.
##       3    1528    3772    4414    6164  100236
3Min Upvotes
4,414Mean Upvotes
100,236Max Upvotes

The top 5 most upvoted words: love, gate rape, fuck, nerd – a nice dispersion of TRUE/FALSE across our indicator variables.

Model Set-Up

Now that we have a better feel for our data, we can start building effective models to predict what makes a good Urban Dictionary word.

Training / Testing Split

We split our data into two sets: 70% training and 30% testing. We set a seed for reproducibility and stratify on up to ensure consistent distribution.

set.seed(729)

urban_dictionary_split <- initial_split(urban_dictionary_data, prop = 0.7, strata = up)
urban_dictionary_train <- training(urban_dictionary_split)
urban_dictionary_test  <- testing(urban_dictionary_split)
## "The training set has 69.94 % of the data, and the testing set has 30.06 % of the data."

Recipe Set-Up

A 'recipe' is a crafted set of instructions that prepares our raw data for consumption by our predictive models. Our predictors – has_tags, political, and explicit – are all categorical, so we dummy code them.

urban_dictionary_recipe <- recipe(up ~ has_tags + political + explicit, 
                                   data = urban_dictionary_train) %>%
  step_mutate(has_tags = as.factor(has_tags)) %>%
  step_mutate(political = as.factor(political)) %>%
  step_mutate(explicit = as.factor(explicit)) %>%
  step_dummy(all_predictors())

K-Fold Cross Validation

With k=10 folds, the dataset is divided into 10 equal parts. The model is trained and tested 10 times, averaging results for a better performance estimate. We stratify on up.

urban_dictionary_folds <- vfold_cv(urban_dictionary_train, v = 10, strata = up)

Fitting The Models

We fit 5 different models: KNN, Lasso, Elastic Net, Random Forest, and Boosted Trees. We use RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error) as our performance metric – lower is better.

KNN  ·  Lasso  ·  Elastic Net  ·  Random Forest  ·  Boosted Trees

Step 1 — Specify Model

# K-NEAREST NEIGHBOR
knn_model <- nearest_neighbor(neighbors = tune()) %>%
  set_mode("regression") %>% set_engine("kknn")

# LASSO
lasso_model <- linear_reg(penalty = tune(), mixture = 1) %>%
  set_mode("regression") %>% set_engine("glmnet")

# ELASTIC NET
elastic_model <- linear_reg(penalty = tune(), mixture = tune()) %>%
  set_mode("regression") %>% set_engine("glmnet")

# RANDOM FOREST
rf_model <- rand_forest(mtry = tune(), trees = tune(), min_n = tune()) %>%
  set_engine("ranger", importance = "impurity") %>% set_mode("regression")

# BOOSTED TREES
boosted_model <- boost_tree(trees = tune(), learn_rate = tune(), min_n = tune()) %>%
  set_engine("xgboost") %>% set_mode("regression")

Step 2 — Set Up Workflows

knn_workflow     <- workflow() %>% add_model(knn_model)     %>% add_recipe(urban_dictionary_recipe)
lasso_workflow   <- workflow() %>% add_model(lasso_model)   %>% add_recipe(urban_dictionary_recipe)
elastic_workflow <- workflow() %>% add_model(elastic_model) %>% add_recipe(urban_dictionary_recipe)
rf_workflow      <- workflow() %>% add_model(rf_model)      %>% add_recipe(urban_dictionary_recipe)
boosted_workflow <- workflow() %>% add_model(boosted_model) %>% add_recipe(urban_dictionary_recipe)

Step 3 — Tuning Grids

knn_grid     <- grid_regular(neighbors(range = c(1,10)), levels = 5)
penalty_grid <- grid_regular(penalty(range = c(-5,5)), levels = 50)
elastic_grid <- grid_regular(penalty(range = c(-5,5)), mixture(range = c(0,1)), levels = 10)
rf_grid      <- grid_regular(mtry(range = c(1,3)), trees(range = c(200,1000)),
                              min_n(range = c(5,20)), levels = 8)
boosted_grid <- grid_regular(trees(range = c(5,200)), learn_rate(range = c(0.01,0.1),
                              trans = identity_trans()), min_n(range = c(40,60)), levels = 5)

Step 4 — Tune Models

knn_tune      <- tune_grid(knn_workflow,     resamples = urban_dictionary_folds, grid = knn_grid)
lasso_tune    <- tune_grid(lasso_workflow,   resamples = urban_dictionary_folds, grid = penalty_grid)
elastic_tune  <- tune_grid(elastic_workflow, resamples = urban_dictionary_folds, grid = elastic_grid)
rf_tune_res   <- tune_grid(rf_workflow,      resamples = urban_dictionary_folds, grid = rf_grid)
boosted_tune_res <- tune_grid(boosted_workflow, resamples = urban_dictionary_folds, grid = boosted_grid)

Step 5 — Collect Metrics

knn_rmse     <- collect_metrics(knn_tune)     %>% filter(.metric == "rmse") %>% summarise(mean_rmse = mean(mean)) %>% pull(mean_rmse)
lasso_rmse   <- collect_metrics(lasso_tune)   %>% filter(.metric == "rmse") %>% summarise(mean_rmse = mean(mean)) %>% pull(mean_rmse)
elastic_rmse <- collect_metrics(elastic_tune) %>% filter(.metric == "rmse") %>% summarise(mean_rmse = mean(mean)) %>% pull(mean_rmse)
rf_rmse      <- collect_metrics(rf_tune_res)  %>% filter(.metric == "rmse") %>% summarise(mean_rmse = mean(mean)) %>% pull(mean_rmse)
boosted_rmse <- collect_metrics(boosted_tune_res) %>% filter(.metric == "rmse") %>% summarise(mean_rmse = mean(mean)) %>% pull(mean_rmse)

Model Results

Results Table

ModelMean RMSE
K-Nearest Neighbors4943.085
Lasso4635.068
Elastic Net4614.729
Random Forest ✓4610.621
Boosted Trees4893.188

Results Graph

Random Forest and Elastic Net performed best. We take both forward for deeper exploration.

Autoplot

Autoplots visualize the effects of hyperparameters on RMSE for our top models.

## # A tibble: 1 × 4
##    mtry trees min_n .config
##   <int> <int> <int> <chr>
## 1     2   542    17 Preprocessor1_Model155

Best performing model: mtry = 2, trees = 542, min_n = 17.

Putting the Best Models to Test

Performance on Folds

## # A tibble: 1 × 9
##    mtry trees min_n .metric .estimator  mean     n std_err .config
##   <int> <int> <int> <chr>   <chr>      <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>
## 1     2   542    17 rmse    standard   4608.    10    433. Preprocessor1_Model1…
Winner: Random Forest #155 – 2 predictors, 542 trees, min_n of 17, RMSE of 4608.096 on cross-validation.

Fitting to Training Data

best_rf_train <- select_best(rf_tune_res, metric = 'rmse')
rf_final_workflow_train <- finalize_workflow(rf_workflow, best_rf_train)
rf_final_fit <- fit(rf_final_workflow_train, data = urban_dictionary_train)

Testing the Model

urban_dictionary_tibble <- predict(rf_final_fit,
                                    new_data = urban_dictionary_test %>% select(-up))
urban_dictionary_tibble <- bind_cols(urban_dictionary_tibble,
                                      urban_dictionary_test %>% select(up))
urban_dictionary_tibble_metrics <- urban_dictionary_metric(urban_dictionary_tibble,
                                                             truth = up, estimate = .pred)
## # A tibble: 1 × 3
##   .metric .estimator .estimate
##   <chr>   <chr>          <dbl>
## 1 rmse    standard       3627.
4,608CV RMSE
3,627Test RMSE ✓
↓ BetterOn Unseen Data

The model performed better on our testing set, yielding a lower RMSE value!

Variable Importance Chart

From this plot, has_tags was the most important predictor in forecasting upvotes, followed by explicit, and largely trailed by political.

Conclusion

Final Thoughts

After fitting our 5 models, we found that the Random Forest Model yielded the most accuracy on predicting the upvotes of an Urban Dictionary word – though admittedly still a poor-performing model overall. It's not surprising that Random Forest came out on top, as it's nonparametric and more flexible. The worst performing model by far was K-Nearest Neighbors.

In the process of categorizing words into political and explicit categories, there were inherent imperfections. If I were to take this project further, I would look into natural language processing (NLP) techniques for a more automated labeling system. Additionally, exploring more specific categories beyond political and explicit could improve virality prediction.

Key takeaway: Whether a word was posted with tags was the single strongest predictor of upvotes – hashtags get words on more pages, reaching more eyes, and driving engagement.

Overall, even though the model wasn't perfect, I feel a huge sense of accomplishment in my growth of machine learning knowledge. These skills and techniques will be so useful as I take on more ML projects!

Sarah Feuer

Predicting Virality of Urban Dictionary Terms
